# pintr

> pintr generates images with the Codex image model (GPT Image, gpt-5.6-terra)
> using your own ChatGPT login, and exposes it over the Model Context Protocol
> (MCP). It runs as a hosted multi-user service and as a local stdio server.

- Source: https://github.com/giulianoo0/pintr
- Hosted service: https://pintr.giuli.dev
- MCP endpoint: https://pintr.giuli.dev/mcp
- License / contributing: see the repo (LLM contributions welcome, human-reviewed)

pintr never asks for an API key. You sign in with a ChatGPT account through
OAuth; the driver model is fixed server-side (gpt-5.6-terra) so callers cannot
pass a different or bogus model.

## Modes

- **Hosted (HTTP, multi-user):** users register (email + password), link one or
  more ChatGPT accounts from a dashboard, and connect MCP clients via the
  standard MCP OAuth flow. Generated images and reference uploads are encrypted
  at rest in S3-compatible storage (Cloudflare R2). This is https://pintr.giuli.dev.
- **Local (stdio, single user):** the client starts the pintr binary; it logs in
  with a local browser flow and saves tokens to `~/.config/pintr/auth.json`.
  Generated images are written to a pintr-chosen cache path returned as `saved_path`.

## Connecting an MCP client

Point the client at the MCP endpoint by URL; no token is pasted into config:

```
claude mcp add --transport http pintr https://pintr.giuli.dev/mcp
```

On first use the client gets a 401, discovers the OAuth endpoints, and opens the
browser. You log in to pintr and click allow; the client receives its own token
(auto-refreshed). Any MCP client with remote support works (Claude Code, Claude
Desktop custom connector, Codex).

## Connecting with an access key manually (no OAuth flow)

If you don't want the browser OAuth flow, use a personal access key as a static
bearer header. Both auth methods work on `/mcp` and `/upload`.

1. Create an account and, on the dashboard, click "new access key". Copy it
   (`pintr_...`) — it is shown only once.
2. Add the server with the key as an `Authorization: Bearer` header:

Claude Code:
```
claude mcp add --transport http pintr https://pintr.giuli.dev/mcp \
  --header "Authorization: Bearer pintr_YOURKEY"
```

Any client that runs a command (Claude Desktop config, etc.), via the mcp-remote
bridge:
```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "pintr": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["-y", "mcp-remote", "https://pintr.giuli.dev/mcp",
               "--header", "Authorization: Bearer pintr_YOURKEY"]
    }
  }
}
```

Scripts / curl send the same header directly:
```
curl -H "Authorization: Bearer pintr_YOURKEY" ... https://pintr.giuli.dev/mcp
```

The key maps to your account and never expires until you revoke it from the
dashboard. Keep it secret — anyone with it can generate on your linked accounts.

## MCP tools

### generate_image
Generate an image. Input:
- `prompt` (string, required) — the full image prompt.
- `reference_images` (string[], optional) — images to anchor a character or
  style, passed as short handles. Do NOT base64-encode or inline images — upload
  the RAW bytes to POST /upload (see below) and pass the returned `ref_...`
  handle. Stdio: pass file paths. References are sent to Codex for the one
  request and never stored; hosted uploads are deleted immediately after use.

Result (hosted): `asset_url` (presigned ciphertext), `decrypted_asset_url` (open
in a browser to view the decrypted PNG), `decryption_key`, `mime_type`, `model`,
`account`, `duration_ms`, `size_bytes`, and `usage` (the account's remaining
rate limits). Always open `decrypted_asset_url` to view the result.
Result (stdio): `saved_path` plus the same metadata.

### get_usage
Return the remaining Codex rate limits for the linked account(s): the 5h, weekly
and monthly windows (only the ones OpenAI currently exposes), each with used and
remaining percent. Result: `{ "accounts": [ { account, plan_type, windows:[{label, used_percent, remaining_percent, resets_at}] } ] }`.

## HTTP endpoints

MCP + OAuth (RFC 9728 protected-resource metadata, RFC 8414 AS metadata, RFC
7591 dynamic client registration, authorization-code + PKCE, RFC 8707 audience):

- `POST /mcp` — MCP streamable HTTP endpoint (bearer required; OAuth access token
  or `pintr_...` access key). Returns 401 with `WWW-Authenticate` when unauthed.
- `GET /.well-known/oauth-protected-resource` (and `/.well-known/oauth-protected-resource/mcp`) — resource metadata.
- `GET /.well-known/oauth-authorization-server` — authorization server metadata.
- `POST /register` — dynamic client registration.
- `GET|POST /authorize` — authorization endpoint (requires a logged-in session; shows a consent page).
- `POST /token` — token endpoint (authorization_code and refresh_token grants).

Assets (all content encrypted at rest; keys are returned once and never stored):

- `POST /upload` — bearer-authed. Upload a raw reference image; the server
  encrypts it, stores only ciphertext, and returns `{ "ref": "ref_<id>.<key>" }`.
  Pass that handle in `generate_image`; it is fetched, decrypted, and deleted
  from storage on use. Max 25 MiB; body must be an image.
- `GET /view?o=<objectKey>&k=<key>` — fetches a stored ciphertext, decrypts it
  server-side, and returns the raw image (`image/png`). This is what
  `decrypted_asset_url` points to.

Dashboard (browser, session cookie + CSRF):

- `GET /` — landing (redirects to /dashboard when signed in).
- `GET|POST /signup` — create an account (no key is minted at signup).
- `GET|POST /login`, `POST /logout` — session auth.
- `GET /dashboard` — manage linked ChatGPT accounts (with rate-limit bars),
  access keys, stored assets, and account deletion.
- `POST /link/start`, `POST /link/finish` — link a ChatGPT account (browser paste flow).
- `POST /accounts/default`, `POST /accounts/remove` — manage linked accounts.
- `POST /keys/create`, `POST /keys/remove` — personal access keys.
- `POST /tokens/revoke` — invalidate all issued OAuth tokens (per-user kill switch).
- `POST /assets/purge` — delete all stored images (and uploads) for the user.
- `POST /account/delete` — permanently delete the account and everything it owns
  (linked accounts, keys, sessions, and all stored objects in S3).
- `GET /llms.txt` — this document.

## Encryption model

- ChatGPT tokens are stored encrypted at rest (AES-256-GCM, key derived from the
  server secret which lives only in the environment). Passwords use argon2id.
- Each generated image and each reference upload is encrypted under a *fresh
  random AES-256-GCM key*. Only ciphertext is stored; the key is returned once in
  the response (or embedded in the upload handle) and never persisted. The bucket
  and pintr itself only ever hold ciphertext they cannot read.
- The encryption protects data at rest (bucket operator, backups, a stolen DB),
  not against a compromised pintr process — pintr necessarily sees each image in
  memory at creation time.

## Self-hosting

Environment (see `.env.example`): `PINTR_PUBLIC_URL`, `PINTR_DB` (SQLite),
`PINTR_SECRET` (>= 32 random chars; signs tokens and encrypts stored
credentials — keep it stable), and `PINTR_S3_*` (`PINTR_S3_ENDPOINT`,
`PINTR_S3_BUCKET`, `PINTR_S3_ACCESS_KEY_ID`, `PINTR_S3_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY`,
`PINTR_S3_REGION`, and optional `PINTR_S3_PUBLIC_BASE` for a bucket-bound custom
domain). Run with `pintr -http 127.0.0.1:8090` behind an HTTPS reverse proxy
with response buffering off. Without `PINTR_S3_*` the server runs but image
generation is disabled.
